The flowchart significantly contradicts the documented sequence of events in multiple ways.

1) Location and method of obtaining the trophy:
- Flowchart step E: "Reveal and open secret compartment to find the trophy" and step F: "Use the trophy to anger the narrator further" imply the trophy is found in a secret compartment on the title screen before the Pong/brick‑breaker segment.
- The wikiHow walkthrough clearly states the trophy only appears after you finish the brick‑breaker game, not from any secret compartment:
  - "When all the blocks are destroyed, a gleaming trophy will appear in the middle of the screen. Click on it to make it drop, then pick it up and drag it over to the blinking green arrow on the right side of the screen. You'll be taken back to the title screen, which is where you'll start the next puzzle." (wikiHow)
- The same source explains that the secret compartment revealed by unscrewing the panel contains the letter board to spell "GAME", not a trophy: "With the letters on the ground, all that will be left in the middle of the screen is 2 screws. Unscrew them... A panel will open on the screen and reveal a letter board that reads 'THERE IS A' with 4 blank spaces after it. Drag and drop the letters that spell 'GAME'..." There is no mention of a trophy there.
- Therefore, the flowchart’s claim that a secret compartment yields the trophy is explicitly contradicted by the evidence.

2) Using the trophy to reach the goat scene:
- Flowchart: F → G → H suggests you use the trophy to "break the narrator's block" and thereby reach the goat and locked cage.
- Evidence: The transition to the goat scene is done by spelling "GOAT" on the letter board after returning from the Pong game with the trophy, not by using the trophy to break anything:
  - "When you return to the title screen after beating the Pong game, drop the trophy. This will cause the screen to 'shake' and the letters on the letter board to fall on the ground. Pick up the letters that spell 'GOAT' and arrange them on the letter board, then click the green arrow that appears on the right side of the screen." (wikiHow)
- The trophy is just dropped to shake the screen and knock letters down; it is not used to break a "block" belonging to the narrator. The flowchart’s description is inaccurate and not supported by the walkthrough.

3) How you obtain and crack the nut:
- Flowchart step J: "Create a nut by knocking down the tree with the trophy" and step K: "Open the nut on a stone or hard surface" are inconsistent with the documented method.
- Evidence shows the nut already exists on the grown tree and is cracked with the metal box, not created by knocking down the tree or opened on some generic hard surface:
  - "After returning to the title screen, a flying squirrel with a key will appear... To do that, replace the letters that spell 'GOAT' on the letter board with the letters that spell 'TREE.' A tree will appear..." (wikiHow)
  - "Pick up the trophy and hover on the right-hand green arrow to go back to the cave where the goat is still trapped in its cage. Hold the trophy underneath the waterfall to fill it up with water. Then drag it back to the title screen and drop it above the tree. Repeat this process 3 times to make the tree grow tall enough..." (wikiHow)
  - "When the top of the tree grows to reach the top of the screen... Find a brown nut on the right side of the tree top and click it until it falls down to the title screen. Follow it down, then click on the metal box from the first puzzle, drag it over the top of the nut, and drop it. The box will crack the nut's shell." (wikiHow)
- Thus, the nut is not "created by knocking down the tree with the trophy", and it is specifically cracked with the metal box, not just any stone/hard surface.

4) Using the trophy in the squirrel/key sequence:
- Flowchart edge from I to J is labeled "the trophy", implying the trophy is the required item to obtain the nut.
- Evidence: The trophy is only used as a water container to grow the tree; the nut itself is obtained by clicking it at the top of the tree and then cracked with the metal box. The key step to get the nut is growing the tree via repeated watering, not directly "knocking down the tree with the trophy".

4. Because none of the flowchart’s steps are explicitly contradicted by the provided documents, and several are directly supported, the flowchart should be judged true under the given criteria.

The flowchart becomes factually incorrect starting at node D.

According to the Stardew Valley Wiki entry for Secret Note #19, the note’s solution is:
- “This is a depiction of 1 Willow Lane with a series of arrows. Starting at the green square depicted, which is directly in front of the door to the house, follow each arrow until the character cannot move anymore. … The path will lead the player around the house, through town, and ultimately to a secret Solid Gold Lewis statue hidden behind Mayor Lewis' house.”
- The reward is explicitly described as “a secret Solid Gold Lewis statue hidden behind Mayor Lewis' house,” and the method to obtain it is: “stand on the destination tile and right-click on the ground.”

Contradictions:
1) Node D says: “Interpret the map as your farm layout and farmhouse location.”
   • The wiki explicitly states the map is a depiction of “1 Willow Lane” (Sam’s house in town), not the farm or farmhouse. Thus, interpreting it as the farm layout is contrary to the documented solution.

2) Node E says: “Start at farmhouse front door and follow the directions tile by tile.”
   • The wiki states the starting point is “directly in front of the door to the house” at 1 Willow Lane, not the player’s farmhouse. This is a direct mismatch.

3) Node F→G says: “Dig at the correct spot with a hoe to uncover the '??HMTGF??' statue.”
   • The wiki says the reward is the “Solid Gold Lewis statue,” not the '??HMTGF??' statue.
   • It also specifies: “To find the statue and take it into the player's inventory, simply stand on the destination tile and right-click on the ground.” It does not require using a hoe or digging.

Because these steps conflict with explicit statements in the provided evidence, the flowchart is not consistent with the facts.

This supports, and does not contradict, the idea of a named zombie used to scare villagers to produce golems and a collection system for iron.

Evidence confirms most of the flow, but explicitly contradicts how you return to Argus from your faction city.

Supported parts:
- The wikiHow article states that to unlock Argus you must be at least level 45 and complete Broken Shore intro quests including “Armies of Legionfall” and “Assault on Broken Shore,” after which Archmage Khadgar gives “The Hand of Fate” that sends you to Argus and begins the Argus questline ("In Dalaran, complete 4 quests ... ‘Assault on Broken Shore,’ and finally ‘The Hand of Fate.’ To get to Argus, all you need to do from there is finish ‘The Hand of Fate.’"). This is consistent with nodes A → B → C.
- Once on Argus, you use the Vindicaar and its teleportation network to move between Argus zones (Krokuun, Eredath, Antoran Wastes), matching nodes C → D → E.

Contradiction:
- For returning to Argus, the flowchart says: from Dalaran go to the Violet Citadel, then use "portal to the Vindicaar on Argus from Vereesa Windrunner (Alliance) / Lady Liadrin (Horde)."
- The wikiHow article instead states that after completing the quest "Rendezvous" you get a lightforged beacon at Krasus’ Landing in Dalaran to return to Argus: "Go to Krasus’ Landing and use the lightforged beacon to get to Argus." It further clarifies that if you don’t see that beacon, you must talk to Vereesa Windrunner at the Stormwind City docks (Alliance) or Lady Liadrin at Orgrimmar’s docks (Horde) to return to Argus: "If you’re in the Alliance faction, speak to Veressa Windrunner at the Stormwind City docks to return to Argus. If you’re in the Horde faction, speak to Lady Liadrin at Orgrimmar’s docks to return to Argus." There is no mention of these NPCs being in the Violet Citadel or providing a direct portal there.
- Therefore, the flowchart’s claim that you go to the Violet Citadel in Dalaran and use a portal from Vereesa/Liadrin there is explicitly inconsistent with the evidence, which places those NPCs at the faction docks (Stormwind/Orgrimmar), not in Dalaran, and describes a different return mechanism from Dalaran itself (the lightforged beacon at Krasus’ Landing).

Because at least one step is factually inconsistent with the provided sources, the flowchart as a whole must be judged false.

The wikiHow article explicitly states: "Ensure your character is level 45. Then, travel to Dalaran... In Dalaran, complete 4 quests to get to Argus..." and further clarifies that "Before you can unlock (and travel to) Argus, your character must be at least level 45." The wikiHow article states that after completing prerequisite quests, you acquire and complete the quest "The Hand of Fate" from Archmage Khadgar at the Violet Citadel in Dalaran, not at Krasus' Landing. Krasus' Landing is mentioned as Khadgar's location for the "Armies of Legionfall" quest, but for "The Hand of Fate" it explicitly says you return to Khadgar at the Violet Citadel. Therefore, the target description's claim that you accept "The Hand of Fate" from Khadgar at Krasus' Landing is contradicted by the evidence.

The wikiHow article explains that to get to Argus you must complete the Legion quest chain, including the Broken Shore introduction quests: after finishing 'Uniting the Isles', you speak to Khadgar to do 'Armies of Legionfall' and then 'Assault on Broken Shore'. Only after completing these and returning to Khadgar do you proceed to 'The Hand of Fate' which unlocks Argus. This supports the statement that, if you have not already completed the Broken Shore scenario, you may need to finish it to unlock the initial Broken Shore quests that lead toward Argus access. There is no evidence contradicting this.

The evidence explains that to reach Argus you must complete the Broken Shore introduction quests (including 'Armies of Legionfall' and 'Assault on Broken Shore') and then do the quest 'The Hand of Fate' from Archmage Khadgar, which sends you via the Exodar to the Vindicaar, the draenei ship that serves as your base on Argus. This matches the description that after the Broken Shore intro, following Khadgar's directions to board the Vindicaar grants access to your primary base on Argus. No statement in the evidence contradicts this; it directly supports it.

The evidence explains that to reach Argus you must complete a quest chain culminating in the quest "The Hand of Fate," which transports you to Argus and unlocks the world. It describes this as an introductory sequence to Argus and notes that finishing it results in Argus being unlocked and accessible. This aligns with the description that completing the scenario that transports you to Argus lets you experience the introductory sequence and arrive in the Argus zone. No part of the evidence contradicts this.

The evidence explains that Argus, introduced in patch 7.3.0, has three main zones—Krokuun, Eredath, and the Antoran Wastes—and that there is an Argus storyline (campaign) involving traveling to Argus and progressing through its quests. It does not state anything that contradicts the claim that by following the Argus questline you can progress through these zones and advance the Argus story. Therefore, the description is consistent with the provided evidence.

The evidence describes Argus as having three main zones—Krokuun, Eredath, and the Antoran Wastes—and explains that progressing through the Argus questline unlocks lightforged beacons/teleport options (a teleportation network) used to travel to and within Argus. Nothing in the documents contradicts the claim that, via the Vindicaar’s teleportation network unlocked through Argus quests, you can move between these three Argus zones. Therefore, the description is consistent with the evidence.

The evidence explains that to return to Argus you should go to Dalaran (e.g., via Dalaran Hearthstone) and then use the lightforged beacon at Krasus’ Landing, which teleports you back to Argus. This matches the description that from Dalaran (Broken Isles) you can access a means (called a portal/teleport) back to Argus. No evidence contradicts this; instead, it explicitly supports it by stating: “Go to Krasus’ Landing and use the lightforged beacon to get to Argus… Completing ‘Rendezvous’ calls down a lightforged beacon on Krasus’ Landing (near Dalaran), which you can use to teleport back to Argus whenever you want!”

The description claims that in World of Warcraft you can travel to Dalaran in the Broken Isles via a portal from a major city such as Stormwind or Orgrimmar to reach the city needed to return to Argus. The wikiHow article states that to get to Dalaran you can take a portal to Azsuna from your capital city (Stormwind City for Alliance, Orgrimmar for Horde) and then fly to Dalaran, or use a portal from Valdrakken, or start the Legion questline, all of which result in reaching Dalaran. It also explains that returning to Argus later is done from Dalaran (via the lightforged beacon at Krasus’ Landing) or via NPCs in Stormwind/Orgrimmar if the beacon isn’t unlocked. Nowhere does the evidence contradict the claim that going to Dalaran (reached from major cities) is how you access the means to return to Argus; instead it supports it. Therefore, the statement is consistent with the evidence.

The wikiHow article explains that to get to Argus you must complete the quest 'The Hand of Fate,' which you obtain from Archmage Khadgar at the Violet Citadel in Dalaran. Completing this quest leads you to Argus and the Vindicaar. It also notes that returning portals/lightforged beacons are unlocked after progressing the Argus questline. This supports the statement that going to the Violet Citadel in Dalaran brings you to the NPC (Khadgar) who effectively provides access (via quest/portal sequence) to the Vindicaar on Argus. No evidence contradicts this description.

The evidence states that Alliance characters can return to Argus by speaking to Vereesa Windrunner at the Stormwind City docks, not in the Violet Citadel in Dalaran, and it does not mention her providing a portal there. The wikiHow article says: "If you’re in the Alliance faction, speak to Veressa Windrunner at the Stormwind City docks to return to Argus." This directly contradicts the claim that she is in the Violet Citadel in Dalaran providing access to a portal to the Vindicaar.

The wikiHow article states that Horde characters can return to Argus by speaking to Lady Liadrin at Orgrimmar’s docks, not in the Violet Citadel in Dalaran, and it does not mention her providing a portal there. The forum thread about returning to the Vindicaar also describes unlocking a Lightforged beacon in Krasus’ Landing in Dalaran or using routes via Exodar/Stormwind/Orgrimmar, but does not mention Lady Liadrin in the Violet Citadel. Therefore, the claim that speaking with Lady Liadrin in the Violet Citadel in Dalaran grants access to a portal to the Vindicaar is contradicted by the evidence.

The evidence explains that Vereesa Windrunner (Alliance) and Lady Liadrin (Horde) can send you back to Argus from the Stormwind or Orgrimmar docks, not from Dalaran. It also states that to travel between Dalaran and the Vindicaar you use a lightforged beacon at Krasus’ Landing in Dalaran after completing the quest 'Rendezvous'. There is no mention of Vereesa or Liadrin offering a portal in Dalaran, and the described Dalaran→Vindicaar travel method explicitly uses the beacon instead. Therefore, the claim that accepting a portal from Vereesa or Liadrin lets you travel from Dalaran to the Vindicaar is contradicted by the evidence.

The evidence describes how to get to Argus and back to the Vindicaar, and mentions Lightforged beacons/portals between Dalaran and the Vindicaar, but it does not say anything about the functionality of the teleportation console on the Vindicaar or about traveling between different regions of Argus using it. There is no explicit statement contradicting the description, so under the given rules (false only if explicitly contradicted), the description should be judged as true.

The evidence explains that after completing the Argus questline up through the quest “Rendezvous,” a lightforged beacon appears at Krasus’ Landing in Dalaran which can be used to return to Argus whenever desired. It also notes alternative NPC-based methods from Stormwind or Orgrimmar docks if the beacon is not yet available. The forum discussion similarly describes unlocking beacons/portals between Dalaran and the Vindicaar via the Argus introductory quests. Nothing in the evidence contradicts the statement that, by following the steps for accessing and returning to Argus (i.e., unlocking the Vindicaar and its related portals/beacons), you can efficiently reach Argus and go back there at any time thereafter. Therefore, the description is consistent with the provided documents.

The evidence describes how to unlock and return to Argus by progressing through specific quests (e.g., completing the Argus storyline through the 11th quest 'Rendezvous' unlocks the lightforged beacon in Dalaran, and completing the Argus campaign grants the 'You Are Now Prepared' achievement and access to allied races like Void Elves and Lightforged Draenei). This supports the idea that progressing and completing Argus quests unlocks additional content and rewards related to the Argus storyline. No statement in the evidence contradicts this description.

The flowchart contains multiple steps that contradict the cited evidence about how Demon Keys and colored keys are obtained and used in Geometry Dash.

1) Demon Keys and Orbs
- Flowchart claim: "Play & Complete Online Levels" → "Collect Orbs (500 orbs → 1 Demon Key)" and then receive a Demon Key every 500 orbs.
- Evidence: The article "How To Get Keys In Geometry Dash?" and the Collectibles page both specify that Demon Keys are obtained per 500 *Mana Orbs*, not generic "orbs", and only orbs from rated levels count. The Collectibles page states: "Demon Keys ... are obtained ... by surpassing an interval of 500 mana orbs" and the mana orb table clarifies how orbs are earned from rated levels. The flowchart’s wording "orbs" and "Online Levels" is imprecise but not explicitly denied; the evidence does not say you *cannot* call them orbs or play online rated levels, so this part is not strictly false under the given standard.

2) Blue Key
- Flowchart claim: You get the Blue Key by: collecting 50 diamonds → accessing the Vault of Secrets → entering the code "The Challenge" → completing a special challenge level → obtaining the Blue Key.
- Evidence: The "How To Get Keys In Geometry Dash?" article states: "The Blue Key is a vital item needed to unlock the Basement in Geometry Dash. You can obtain it as a reward for completing the hidden Chaos Gauntlet, which becomes accessible by clicking the blue lock in the Basement." There is no mention of the Vault of Secrets, the code "The Challenge", or a special challenge level as the method to obtain the Blue Key. Instead, the evidence gives a completely different, explicit method (Chaos Gauntlet). This directly contradicts the flowchart’s procedure.

3) Green Key
- Flowchart claim: From the Vault of Secrets you go to the Basement, enter the code "The Challenge" in the Basement, complete a "Basement Challenge", and obtain the Green Key.
- Evidence: The same article states: "The Green Key is essential for unlocking the Basement in Geometry Dash. You can obtain it by opening the first large chest on the third page of the Treasure Room. However, to unlock this chest, you must first open 50 smaller chests." The Treasure Room page confirms: "The third screen contains three large chests ... with the first large chest also contains the green key needed to remove the green lock in the Basement. First large chest: 50 chests." There is no mention of any Basement challenge or code; the method is explicitly via Treasure Room chests. This directly contradicts the flowchart.

4) Orange Key
- Flowchart claim: From the Vault of Secrets you "Unlock the Chamber of Time" and then, using 500 diamonds, access the Chamber of Time via a lock on the level select screen, enter the code "River" there, and obtain the Orange Key.
- Evidence: The article states: "The Orange Key is the last Key needed to open the Basement. This Key is found in the Chamber of Time, which can be accessed by clicking on the 'ground' in the middle of the 'Coming Soon' screen, which is in the main level slider. You must click the orange lock to be able to click the hidden door on the 'Coming Soon' screen. To open the secret door to the Chamber of Time, you must buy the Master Emblem for 1,000 Mana Orbs from the Secret Shop. The Secret Shop is unlocked by getting to the final screen of the Treasure Room and paying 500 Diamonds." So: (a) access is via Master Emblem (1,000 mana orbs) bought in the Secret Shop, not by spending 500 diamonds directly on the Chamber of Time; (b) the 500 diamonds are for unlocking the Secret Shop, not for entering the Chamber; and (c) no code "River" is mentioned as the condition for obtaining the Orange Key. The flowchart’s steps and costs are therefore explicitly inconsistent with the evidence.

5) Use of Demon Keys in Treasure Room
- Flowchart claim: "Use Demon Keys in the Treasure Room".
- Evidence: The Treasure Room and Collectibles pages confirm that Demon Keys unlock the Treasure Room and its chests: "The Treasure Room ... requiring 5 demon keys to gain access" and "Demon Keys unlock the Treasure Room ... there are six different levels of chests ... cost 1, 5, 10, 25, 50, and 100 keys." This part is consistent.

Because the methods for obtaining the Blue, Green, and Orange Keys in the flowchart are explicitly contradicted by the detailed procedures in the evidence, the flowchart as a whole must be judged false under the stated policy.

The evidence explains that keys (Demon Keys, Gold Keys, and special colored keys) are used to unlock the Treasure Room, its chests, the Basement, and the Demon Gauntlet, which provide rewards and access to special areas. This supports the statement that in Geometry Dash, keys are used to unlock various features and rewards. No evidence contradicts this.

The evidence lists several types of keys in Geometry Dash: Demon Keys, Gold Keys, and special coloured keys (Blue, Green, Orange, Purple). The Collectibles page explicitly distinguishes Demon Keys and Gold Keys as separate types: "Gold Keys are similar to demon keys in that they are used to unlock chests in the Treasure Room..." This contradicts the claim that there are only two main types of keys (Demon and Colored), since Gold Keys are another main key type used for unlocking content.

The evidence states that Demon Keys "unlock the Treasure Room" and are then used to open various tiers of chests there (costing 1, 5, 10, 25, 50, or 100 Demon Keys). These are consistently referred to simply as chests in the Treasure Room, not as "Demon Chests." No sentence in the evidence explicitly contradicts the claim that Demon Keys are used to open Demon Chests in the Treasure Room; it just uses different terminology. Since there is no explicit denial, the description must be judged true according to the given criteria.

The evidence states that Demon Keys are obtained by surpassing intervals of 500 mana orbs, and that mana orbs are earned by making progress in and completing rated (i.e., online) levels. Specifically, the first article notes: "For every 500 Mana Orbs you get from levels, you will get one Demon Key" and clarifies that you must play a rated level to get mana orbs. The Collectibles page also confirms that Demon Keys are obtained by surpassing 500 mana-orb intervals and that mana orbs are accumulated by making forward progress on rated levels. There is no statement contradicting that playing online/rated levels lets you earn orbs which then contribute toward Demon Keys. Therefore, the description is consistent with the evidence.

The evidence states that Demon Keys are obtained by surpassing intervals of 500 mana orbs, and that mana orbs are earned by making progress in rated levels (and also from chests, gauntlets, etc.). It also explicitly clarifies that orbs from daily chests, the treasure room, or gauntlets do not count toward Demon Keys in one source, but the Collectibles page more generally says Demon Keys are obtained from wraith codes, daily chests, event level rewards, or by surpassing 500 mana orbs. The target description claims Demon Keys are earned by collecting orbs obtained by completing online levels. This is consistent with the core mechanic described (500 mana orbs from rated/online levels give 1 Demon Key) and is not contradicted by the evidence; it is just less detailed and omits other sources of Demon Keys. Since the guidelines say to mark as false only if there is an explicit contradiction, and none exists, the description should be judged true.

The evidence states that "For every 500 Mana Orbs you get, you will get 1 Key" and clarifies in the collectibles article that Demon Keys "are obtained ... by surpassing an interval of 500 mana orbs". There is no statement contradicting that 500 orbs (mana orbs) grant one Demon Key; instead, this is explicitly confirmed. Therefore, the description is factually accurate.

The evidence states that mana orbs are obtained by "making forward progress on levels that received a star rating with a difficulty higher than Auto," and provides a table showing that higher difficulties (Easy through Demon) give more orbs per completion. It also notes that unrated levels give 0 stars and thus do not count as rated. The target description claims that completing "any online levels" gives orbs, but the evidence restricts orb rewards to rated levels only, not all online levels. Therefore, the description is contradicted by the evidence.

The evidence states that Demon Keys are obtained "by surpassing an interval of 500 mana orbs" and that "for every 500 Mana Orbs you get, you will get one Demon Key." It also clarifies that these orbs must come from rated levels (online levels) and describes that keys can be stockpiled as you revisit rated levels and gain more orbs. Nothing in the evidence contradicts the claim that by continuing to play online (rated) levels and collecting mana orbs until reaching 500, you automatically receive a Demon Key. Therefore, the description is consistent with the evidence.

The evidence states that the Treasure Room is accessed "through a door in the lower right corner of the Online Menu, requiring 5 demon keys to gain access," not by clicking a treasure chest icon on the main menu. However, the description’s core claim is that the Treasure Room is the place where Demon (demon‑key) chests are opened, which the evidence supports: inside the Treasure Room there are multiple sections of chests costing various numbers of demon keys. The incorrect detail about how you navigate there is not explicitly contradicted (the evidence just gives the correct method), and the main factual point about the Treasure Room being where demon‑key chests are opened is affirmed. Since there is no explicit statement denying the description, it should be judged true under the given rules.

The evidence states that Demon Keys are used to unlock chests in the Treasure Room: 'Demon Keys unlock the Treasure Room, and this is the only place that they can be viewed. There are six different levels of chests... each requiring a different number of keys to open.' It also details that these chests contain various rewards such as orbs, diamonds, shards, icons, and colors. This matches the description that in Geometry Dash you can use Demon Keys in the Treasure Room to open chests (Demon Chests) and obtain rewards. There is no contradicting information.

The evidence explains that the special colored keys (blue, green, orange, purple) are used as follows: the blue, green, and orange keys are "used to remove the locks in the Basement," and the purple key "is used to unlock the Demon Gauntlet." It also states that the green key is obtained from the Treasure Room, the blue key from the Chaos Gauntlet, and the orange key from the Chamber of Time. Nowhere does the evidence say that colored keys are used to unlock the Vault of Secrets or the Chamber of Time itself. In fact, the Chamber of Time is accessed via a hidden door and the Master Emblem, not via a colored key. Thus, the claim that colored keys are used to unlock the Vault of Secrets and the Chamber of Time is not supported and is contradicted by the description of how those areas are accessed.

The evidence states that diamonds are used to unlock the Vault of Secrets, but it does not specify the required number of diamonds or that 50 diamonds is the threshold. More importantly, it clearly explains that the Blue Key is obtained as a reward for completing the hidden Chaos Gauntlet, not from the Vault of Secrets. The first evidence document says: "The Blue Key is a vital item needed to unlock the Basement in Geometry Dash. You can obtain it as a reward for completing the hidden Chaos Gauntlet..." and does not associate the Blue Key with the Vault of Secrets or a 50‑diamond requirement. Therefore, the claim that 50 diamonds unlock the Vault of Secrets and obtain the Blue Key is contradicted by the evidence.

The provided evidence describes how to unlock the Treasure Room, Basement, Chamber of Time, and various key mechanics, but it does not mention the Vault of Secrets or how to access it. There is no explicit statement confirming or denying that clicking a lock icon in the Settings menu with 50 diamonds unlocks the Vault of Secrets. Since the guidelines require an explicit contradiction in the evidence to judge the description as false, and no such contradiction is present, the description cannot be deemed false based on the given documents.

The evidence explains how the Blue Key is obtained: "The Blue Key is a vital item needed to unlock the Basement in Geometry Dash. You can obtain it as a reward for completing the hidden Chaos Gauntlet..." There is no mention anywhere that the Blue Key is related to the level 'The Challenge' or to entering any code in the Vault of Secrets. Separately, the Collectibles page states that diamonds unlock "the secret level The Challenge," but it does not connect this level to the Blue Key. Thus, the claim that typing "The Challenge" in the Vault of Secrets unlocks a challenge level required for obtaining the Blue Key is contradicted by the explicit statement that the Blue Key instead comes from completing the Chaos Gauntlet.

The evidence states that the Blue Key is obtained as a reward for completing the hidden Chaos Gauntlet, not from the level 'The Challenge' or from a code in the Vault of Secrets. Specifically, it says: 'The Blue Key is a vital item needed to unlock the Basement in Geometry Dash. You can obtain it as a reward for completing the hidden Chaos Gauntlet.' There is no mention of the Blue Key being tied to 'The Challenge' or a Vault of Secrets code, so the description contradicts the evidence.

The evidence explains that the Green Key is obtained from the first large chest on the third page of the Treasure Room, unlocked after opening 50 smaller chests: "The third screen contains three large chests... with the first large chest also contains the green key needed to remove the green lock in the Basement." It does not state that going to the Basement via the door in the Vault of Secrets begins the process of obtaining the Green Key, nor is such a connection described. Therefore, the target description is contradicted by the explicit explanation of how the Green Key is actually obtained.

The evidence states that the Green Key is obtained from the first large chest on the third page of the Treasure Room, unlocked after opening 50 smaller chests: "The third screen contains three large chests... with the first large chest also contains the green key needed to remove the green lock in the Basement." There is no mention of typing the code "The Challenge" in the Basement to unlock a challenge for the Green Key, and the Green Key's method of acquisition is explicitly different. Therefore, the description is contradicted by the evidence.

The evidence states that the Green Key is obtained by opening the first large chest on the third page of the Treasure Room after opening 50 smaller chests, and that it is then used to remove the green lock in the Basement. It does not mention any special challenge in the Basement to obtain the Green Key. Therefore, the claim that completing a special challenge unlocked in the Basement grants the Green Key is contradicted by the evidence.

The evidence explains that to access the Chamber of Time (where the Orange Key is found), you must click the hidden door on the 'Coming Soon' screen after buying the Master Emblem from the Secret Shop, which itself is unlocked via the Treasure Room and diamonds. It does not state anywhere that unlocking the Vault of Secrets is a prerequisite for unlocking the Chamber of Time or obtaining the Orange Key, nor does it indicate any dependency between the Vault of Secrets and the Chamber of Time. Therefore, the claim that unlocking the Vault of Secrets is a required prerequisite before unlocking the Chamber of Time for the Orange Key is contradicted by omission and by the explicit alternative prerequisite path described.

The evidence states that to access the Chamber of Time, you must buy the Master Emblem for 1,000 Mana Orbs from the Secret Shop, and that the Secret Shop is unlocked by collecting 500 Diamonds. Thus, 500 diamonds unlock the Secret Shop, not the Chamber of Time itself. The Chamber of Time requires an additional step (purchasing the Master Emblem). Therefore, the claim that 500 diamonds meet the requirement to unlock the Chamber of Time is contradicted by the evidence.

The evidence states that the Chamber of Time is accessed by clicking on the ground in the middle of the 'Coming Soon' screen in the main level slider, after buying the Master Emblem from the Secret Shop. It does not mention accessing it by clicking a lock icon on the right side of the Level Select screen, nor that simply having 500 diamonds grants access. In fact, 500 diamonds are used to unlock the Secret Shop, not the Chamber of Time directly. Therefore, the described method (lock icon + 500 diamonds) contradicts the documented method.

The evidence states that the Orange Key is found in the Chamber of Time and that to open the secret door to the Chamber of Time you must buy the Master Emblem and access the hidden door on the 'Coming Soon' screen. It does not mention any code, and specifically does not say that typing 'River' (or any code) in the Chamber of Time grants the Orange Key. Therefore, the claim that entering the code 'River' in the Chamber of Time gives the Orange Key is not supported and is contradicted by the described method of obtaining the key.

The evidence confirms that daily quests and related activities grant orbs and diamonds, and that Demon Keys are earned by surpassing intervals of 500 mana orbs, as well as from daily chests and other rewards. Specifically, the Collectibles page states that diamonds are obtained by completing daily quests and that mana orbs are obtained from various sources, and it also notes that Demon Keys are obtained "by surpassing an interval of 500 mana orbs" and from daily chests and other rewards. Since more orbs (from quests) contribute toward reaching each 500-orb threshold, participating in these activities does indeed help you obtain Demon Keys faster. No part of the evidence contradicts this description.

The description claims that in Geometry Dash you can use collected Demon Keys by visiting the Treasure Room and opening chests for rewards. The evidence states that Demon Keys are used to unlock the Treasure Room and its chests: 'The Treasure Room ... requiring 5 demon keys to gain access' and that inside, '400 chests costing one demon key each', '100 chests costing 5 demon keys each', etc., and that 'Demon Keys unlock the Treasure Room, and this is the only place that they can be viewed.' These chests 'contain a variety of rewards, including orbs, diamonds, shards, icons and colors.' This directly supports that using Demon Keys in the Treasure Room to open chests and claim rewards is how you make use of them. There is no contradicting statement.

The evidence explains in detail how to obtain Demon Keys (via every 500 Mana Orbs from rated levels, daily chests, etc.) and how to obtain and use the colored keys (Blue, Green, Orange, Purple) to unlock the Basement, Treasure Room, and Demon Gauntlet, leading to new features and rewards. Nothing in the evidence contradicts the statement that by following these described steps you can efficiently collect both types of keys and unlock new content; instead, it directly supports it.

The flowchart describes a general procedure: install Steam and SteamVR, install and log into the Oculus app on a Windows PC, connect Quest 2 via Oculus Link (wired), Air Link (wireless), or Virtual Desktop (optional wireless), then launch SteamVR and play SteamVR games. The wikiHow article explicitly supports installing Steam and SteamVR, installing the Oculus app, and using both wired (Oculus Link) and wireless (Air Link) connections with a Quest 2 to launch SteamVR and play VR games. The Meta community forum post further confirms that Link (USB cable), Air Link (Wi‑Fi), and Virtual Desktop (Wi‑Fi, paid) are all valid methods to access SteamVR content from a Quest 2. None of the evidence states that any of these methods are impossible or incorrect; Virtual Desktop is explicitly mentioned as an alternative that can access the SteamVR library. There is no explicit contradiction in the sources to any step or connection in the flowchart, including the idea that SteamVR will detect the Quest 2 via these methods and allow playing SteamVR games.

The evidence repeatedly states that to play Steam VR games on an Oculus Quest 2 you must use a computer with Steam and SteamVR installed. The wikiHow article specifies downloading Steam and SteamVR on a Windows 10 or 11 PC before playing, and the Linux-focused article also requires installing SteamVR. None of the documents suggest that SteamVR games can be played on a Quest 2 without a VR-capable PC running Steam and SteamVR. There is no explicit contradiction to the target description.

The target description states that to play SteamVR games on an Oculus Quest 2, an Oculus Quest 2 headset is required. The evidence documents all describe methods for playing SteamVR games specifically using an Oculus Quest 2 headset (via Oculus Link, Air Link, ALVR, Steam Link, etc.), and none of them claim or imply that SteamVR games on a Quest 2 can be played without having a Quest 2 headset. There is no explicit contradiction to the statement; instead, the procedures assume the presence of a Quest 2 as necessary hardware. Therefore, the description is consistent with the evidence.

The wikiHow article explicitly states that to use a wired connection you should "Use a long USB-C to connect your headset to your computer," and the Meta forum thread lists "Link: usb cable" as the cable-based method for Quest PCVR. Another user mentions using a third-party "Kiwi 16ft with signal amplifier" USB cable. These sources support that a wired connection for SteamVR gaming with Quest 2 uses either the official Oculus Link cable or another good‑quality USB‑C cable. There is no evidence contradicting this requirement.

The wikiHow article explicitly states that to connect an Oculus Quest 2 to a PC for playing SteamVR games, you need to download and use the Oculus app on your Windows 10 or 11 computer: "Download the Oculus app onto your computer. This program is needed to connect your Oculus headset to your computer." No evidence contradicts this requirement; other documents discuss alternative tools (ALVR, Steam Link) but do not deny that for the standard Link/Air Link setup, the Oculus app is required. Therefore, the description is consistent with the evidence.

The evidence states that for wireless PCVR with a Quest 2 (using Air Link or similar), you need a suitable Wi‑Fi network, specifically recommending 5 GHz Wi‑Fi or Ethernet for the PC and that the headset and PC be on the same network. For example, the wikiHow article lists as a requirement: "Internet: 5 GHZ Wi-Fi or Ethernet connection," and the Linux/ALVR guide says the headset must be connected to Wi‑Fi and the PC to the same network, preferably wired, with the Wi‑Fi broadcasting on 5 GHz. These statements support, and do not contradict, the claim that a compatible Wi‑Fi network is required for using Oculus Air Link or Virtual Desktop with a Quest 2.

The evidence shows that to play Steam VR games on an Oculus Quest 2, you must have Steam and SteamVR installed on a capable PC. The wikiHow article states: "You should already have Steam and any VR-compatible game downloaded to your computer" and then "Download SteamVR. You can find this in the Steam library. Search for 'SteamVR' and download it to your computer." This is fully consistent with the description that downloading and installing Steam on a VR-ready PC prepares it to run SteamVR and SteamVR games for use with an Oculus Quest 2. No evidence contradicts this claim.

The evidence explains that to play Steam VR games on an Oculus Quest 2 you need Steam and SteamVR installed on a VR-capable PC, and that SteamVR is obtained from and launched via Steam on the PC (e.g., “You should already have Steam and any VR-compatible game downloaded to your computer… Download SteamVR. You can find this in the Steam library. Search for 'SteamVR' and download it to your computer.”). This supports the statement that opening Steam on a VR‑ready PC gives access to the Steam store and library needed to install and launch SteamVR for use with an Oculus Quest 2. No evidence contradicts this.

The wikiHow article states: "Download SteamVR. You can find this in the Steam library. Search for 'SteamVR' and download it to your computer." It also explains that SteamVR is needed to play Steam VR games on an Oculus Quest 2. This directly supports the description that by searching for 'SteamVR' in the Steam store you can find the SteamVR application required to run SteamVR games on an Oculus Quest 2. No evidence contradicts this.

The wikiHow article explicitly instructs users to "Download SteamVR. You can find this in the Steam library. Search for 'SteamVR' and download it to your computer" as part of setting up a PC to play Steam VR games on an Oculus Quest 2. It then explains that, once SteamVR is installed and launched, you can select and run VR games on the Quest 2 via wired Link or wireless Air Link. This supports the statement that downloading and installing SteamVR from the Steam store on a VR‑ready PC enables the PC to run SteamVR and interface with an Oculus Quest 2. No evidence contradicts this.

The wikiHow article explicitly states: "Download the Oculus app onto your computer. You can find the download on the official website here. This program is needed to connect your Oculus headset to your computer." This directly supports the description that downloading and installing the Oculus app from the official Oculus website on a PC provides the software needed to manage and connect the Oculus Quest 2 to the PC. No evidence contradicts this.

The wikiHow article explicitly instructs users to 'Download the Oculus app onto your computer' and later, for both wired and wireless methods, to 'Open the Oculus app on your computer' or 'Launch the Oculus app' as the step used to connect and configure the Oculus Quest 2 with the PC. This supports the statement that launching the Oculus app on your PC is how you begin configuring the connection between the PC and the Quest 2. No evidence contradicts this.

The wikiHow article explains that for a wired connection you connect the Quest 2 to the PC with a USB‑C cable, launch the Oculus app on the PC, add the headset via Link (Cable), and then the PC desktop appears in the headset, allowing you to launch SteamVR and PC VR games. The evidence confirms many parts of the flowchart but explicitly contradicts one key element.

Supported steps:
- Large Stumps: The wiki states hardwood "is obtained by chopping down ... a Large Stump with a Copper Axe or better" and that six Large Stumps in the Secret Woods respawn daily, yielding 12 hardwood per day. This matches nodes A1–A3 and C1–C4 (Copper Axe or better for Large Stumps, 6 stumps → 12 hardwood/day, Secret Woods access via removing the blocking log with a Steel Axe).
- Large Logs: The wiki states hardwood is obtained by chopping "a Large Log with a Steel Axe or better," matching B1–B3.
- Mahogany Trees: The wiki states hardwood is obtained by chopping down a Mahogany Tree with any axe, and the forum thread confirms Mahogany seeds come from Secret Woods stumps and can be planted and sped up with Tree Fertilizer. This supports D1–D4 and D5–D7 (seeds from stumps/trees, planting, optional Tree Fertilizer, any axe for hardwood).
- Mines/Skull Cavern containers: The wiki says, "Hardwood can also be received as loot by breaking barrels and boxes in the Mines or Skull Cavern," matching F1–F2.

Contradicted step:
- Purchasing from Robin: The flowchart includes step E1→E2: "Visit Robin's Carpenter's Shop" → "Purchase hardwood if available (not cost‑effective)." The provided Hardwood page lists all sources of hardwood (Large Stump, Large Log, Mahogany Tree, Mystic Tree, Mystery Box, Woodskip Fish Pond, Bookseller, Secret Woods, Ginger Island, Dangerous Mines, Mines/Skull Cavern crates, Robin's Feast of the Winter Star gift, pet dog, Mystery Box, Desert Festival, Bookseller trade, etc.) and does not mention any ability to buy hardwood directly from Robin's Carpenter's Shop. Instead, it only lists hardwood as a *cost* in Robin's building upgrades (e.g., "Farmhouse Upgrade 2 ... 65,000g Hardwood (100)", "Stable ... 10,000g Hardwood (100)", "Pet Bowl ... 5,000g Hardwood (25)"). There is no statement that Robin sells hardwood. The only explicit purchase source in the evidence is Leah's shop at the Desert Festival: "Up to 100 Hardwood can be purchased from Leah's shop at the Desert Festival for 3 Calico Eggs each." Because the evidence enumerates purchase options and names Leah (not Robin) as a seller, and ties Robin only to using hardwood as a required material, the flowchart's claim that you can purchase hardwood from Robin is inconsistent with the documented facts.

Since at least one element (purchasing hardwood from Robin) is contradicted by the evidence, the flowchart as a whole must be judged false.

The Stardew Valley Wiki page explicitly states that hardwood is a resource and lists numerous crafting recipes (e.g., Cheese Press, Oil Maker, fences, Warp Totems, etc.) and building upgrades (e.g., Farmhouse Upgrade 2, Stable, Pet Bowl) that require hardwood. This directly supports the description that hardwood is a valuable resource used for crafting and building upgrades, and there is no contradictory information in the evidence.

The Stardew Valley Wiki explicitly states: "Hardwood is a resource. It is obtained by chopping down ... a Large Stump with a Copper Axe or better." Both sources support that regularly visiting the Secret Woods and continuously growing Mahogany Trees provide a steady, long-term supply of hardwood, and nothing in the evidence contradicts this.

The flowchart describes a high-level procedure for using incantations in Elden Ring: obtain an incantation, ensure you meet stat requirements, use a Sacred Seal, memorize the incantation at a Site of Grace, equip the Seal, select the incantation, cast it, and manage FP/stamina. The evidence from both SteelSeries and wikiHow confirms all of these steps:

- You must have an incantation in your inventory (acquire an incantation): wikiHow step 1 explicitly states you need to have the incantation and explains how to obtain them.
- Incantations have stat requirements, especially Faith (check required stats): SteelSeries notes “Ensure you have sufficient Faith to use the incantation” and wikiHow mentions spells have requirements that can prevent casting.
- You must equip a Sacred Seal to cast incantations: SteelSeries step 1 and wikiHow step 3 both state that a Sacred Seal is required.
- You must memorize/equip incantations at a Site of Grace in spell/memory slots: SteelSeries step 2 and wikiHow step 2 & FAQ #3 explain using ‘Memorize Spells’ at a Site of Grace and memory slots.
- You then equip the Sacred Seal in a hand slot (main or off-hand): SteelSeries step 1 and wikiHow’s instructions on equipping a Seal in your equipment menu confirm this.
- You select the desired incantation via D-pad/keyboard (select the incantation): SteelSeries step 3 and wikiHow step 4 describe using the D-pad or ↑ key to cycle/select incantations.
- You cast the incantation using the attack button while the Seal is equipped (cast the incantation): SteelSeries step 4 and wikiHow step 4 give the exact buttons for casting.
- Casting consumes FP and is limited by your resource pool; managing FP with flasks is standard gameplay and not contradicted anywhere. The guides mention Mind/FP and flask upgrades (Sacred Tears, Memory Stones, etc.), which is consistent with “manage FP and stamina (-> Flask of Cerulean Tears).”
- Experimenting and adapting with different incantations is general advice and not contradicted; wikiHow and SteelSeries both encourage trying different spells and note their varying ranges/effects.

No part of the flowchart asserts anything that is explicitly contradicted by the evidence. The ordering (memorize at a Site of Grace, then equip and use a Sacred Seal to cast) matches the sources, and no step is denied or reversed by the documents. Therefore, the flowchart is consistent with the provided evidence.

The description claims that in Elden Ring a player can obtain an incantation (the magical spell) as loot, a boss reward, or by purchasing from merchants or NPCs who teach magic, and that this is how they get what is needed to begin using incantations. The evidence explains that incantations are spells and explicitly states they can be bought from NPC trainers (Brother Corhyn, Miriel, D, Gowry, Gurranq) and also found in prayer books or looted from enemies. This matches the description's mention of purchasing from merchants/NPCs and acquiring as loot. The evidence does not contradict the statement that such acquisition provides the spell needed to start using incantations; it only adds that you also need a Sacred Seal and to memorize the spell, which does not negate the claim about how you obtain the spell itself. There is no explicit contradiction in the documents.

The evidence describes incantations in Elden Ring as spells that can be bought from various NPC trainers/merchants (Brother Corhyn, Miriel, D, Gowry, Gurranq, etc.), looted from the world (e.g., found in prayer books, chests, or as items in areas), and obtained as rewards for defeating bosses (e.g., Dragon Hearts from dragons can be traded for dragon incantations; bosses like Dragonkin Soldier of Nokstella drop specific incantations such as Frozen Lightning Spear; Commander O'Neil and other bosses are tied to incantation-related quest items). This matches the target description that incantations are magical spells obtainable as loot, boss rewards, or purchases from merchants/NPCs. No part of the evidence contradicts this.

The evidence explains that incantations in Elden Ring have stat requirements (primarily Faith, sometimes others) and that you cannot cast an incantation if you do not meet those requirements (e.g., the spell icon shows an 'x' if requirements are not met). It also notes that Faith and Mind should be leveled to meet incantation requirements. This supports the idea that checking an incantation’s stat requirements and confirming the character meets them is how a player ensures the character can cast that incantation. Nothing in the evidence contradicts this description.

The evidence repeatedly states that to cast incantations in Elden Ring, the player must equip a Sacred Seal, which is the casting tool for incantations (e.g., "In order to cast any spell or incantation, you must have a sacred seal equipped" and "Prophet and Confessor are the only classes that start with a Seal and incantations"). This directly supports the description that equipping a Sacred Seal provides the casting tool required to perform incantations. No evidence contradicts this.

The evidence repeatedly explains that Sacred Seals are required to cast incantations, are equipped like weapons in a hand slot, and that there are multiple types which can be purchased from vendors (e.g., Finger Seal from Twin Maiden Husks) or obtained via starting classes or exploration. This matches the description that Sacred Seals function as casting tools for incantations, similar to weapons, and can be found or purchased throughout the game. No evidence contradicts this.

The evidence explains that to use incantations you must equip them in memory slots via a Site of Grace: rest at a Site of (Lost) Grace, select 'Memorize Spells', and choose the incantations to add to your memory slots. This matches the description that interacting with a Site of Grace and using 'Memorize Spells' makes the chosen incantations available in spell slots. No evidence contradicts this.

The evidence explains that to use incantations in Elden Ring, you must first equip them in your memory slots via the 'Memorize Spells' option at a Site of Grace (e.g., wikiHow: "Equip the incantation you want to use at a Site of Lost Grace" and SteelSeries: "Rest at a Site of Grace. Select 'Memorize Spells.' Choose the incantations you want to add to your memory slots."). This directly supports the description that equipping a memorized incantation in this menu readies it for use in combat or exploration. No part of the evidence contradicts this.

The description states that in Elden Ring you must equip a Sacred Seal in either main hand or off-hand so it can be used to cast incantations. For example, the SteelSeries guide states: “Press the light attack button (R1 for right hand or L1 for left hand, depending on where your seal is equipped) to cast the selected incantation.” The wikiHow article similarly says: “For PS5… press the right bumper to cast your spell. For Xbox… hit R1 to cast it.” This matches the description that you cast the currently selected incantation by pressing the attack button corresponding to the hand with the equipped Sacred Seal (R1/RB or L1/LB). There is no contradicting information.

The evidence documents explain how to use incantations (equip a Sacred Seal, memorize spells, select them, and press the attack button) and mention requirements like having sufficient Faith and Mind/FP. The evidence describes how incantations in Elden Ring work, explicitly stating that they have a variety of effects such as healing, curing status effects, dealing damage, and enchanting people or weapons. The flowchart steps are consistent with the evidence and not contradicted by it. Both evidence articles describe essentially the same procedure: (1) register/create a developer account and organization on the Oculus/Meta developer site, (2) ensure the Oculus/Meta mobile app is installed and you are logged in with the same verified developer account, (3) select your Quest 2 device in the app (i.e., pair/connect it), (4) enable Developer Mode via the app’s Developer Mode toggle, and (5) optionally connect the headset to a computer with USB and allow USB debugging to use it from a PC. The first article states: “The first thing you need to do is register as a developer on the Oculus website… you can enable developer mode from the Oculus app on your phone. Just go to Settings, then Devices, and select your Quest 2… you’ll see an option to enable developer mode.” It also describes connecting to a computer afterward. The second article similarly outlines: Step 1: Register as a Developer; Step 2: Enable Developer Mode using the Oculus app or Developer Hub, including selecting the device and toggling Developer Mode. The flowchart’s final step of verifying Developer Mode by connecting to a computer is compatible with the described practice of connecting via USB and using debugging; there is no statement in the evidence that contradicts restarting the headset or verifying via PC connection. Therefore, no explicit contradiction exists, so the flowchart should be judged true.

The evidence states that to enable developer mode on a Quest 2, the first step is to go to the Oculus developer website (e.g., https://developer.oculus.com/manage/organizations/create/), log in, and register/verify as a developer. After that, developer mode can be enabled via the Oculus app or Meta Quest Developer Hub. This supports the description that by visiting the Oculus Developer website a user can begin the process of enabling Developer Mode on an Oculus Quest 2. No evidence contradicts this.

The evidence explains that to enable developer mode you must log in to the Oculus/Meta developer website with your Meta/Facebook/Instagram account, verify it, create a developer organization, and then enable developer mode for your headset via the Oculus mobile app or Meta Quest Developer Hub. The association/activation step is explicitly done later through the app or MQDH, not automatically by website sign‑in alone. Since the described automatic association is not supported and is implicitly contradicted by the required extra steps, the target description is false.

The evidence explains that to enable developer mode on an Oculus/Meta Quest 2, the user must first register as a developer on the Oculus (Meta) developer website, create/verify a developer organization, and then they can enable Developer Mode via the Oculus app or within the headset. The evidence describes the registration process for becoming an Oculus/Meta developer. The evidence explains that to register as a developer you must create a developer organization on the Oculus (Meta) developer website: you log in, verify your account, then "Enter your developer organization’s name" and submit, agreeing to the non‑disclosure agreement. The evidence states that when registering as a developer on the Oculus/Meta developer site, if you are not an app developer you can "just type in whatever you want" for the developer organization’s name. The evidence states that to enable developer mode you must use the Oculus application on your phone, but it does not specify or restrict the phone’s operating system. The evidence states that to turn on developer mode you must use the Oculus/Meta app on your phone and "ensure you are signed in with the same account you just verified" and that the headset is "signed into the same account that is signed into the Oculus applications on the mobile device." The evidence states that to enable developer mode you must use the Oculus app on your phone: "Once you’ve done that, you can enable developer mode from the Oculus app on your phone. The evidence describes using the Oculus (Meta Quest) smartphone app to connect to and manage a Quest 2 headset: you open the Oculus app, go to Menu → Devices, select your headset (which must be on and nearby with Bluetooth/Wi‑Fi), and then proceed with settings like enabling Developer Mode. The evidence explains that after enabling Developer Mode on the Oculus/Meta Quest 2, you can connect the headset to a computer using the USB cable, allow USB debugging, and then "directly push builds from your computer to your headset" or use the Meta Quest Developer Hub device manager. The flowchart claims that on Nintendo Switch you can (1) connect a smartphone via the Just Dance Controller app for a "camera‑controller mode" and (2) have motion tracking via a camera or Joy‑Con. Location of mangrove trees: The Mangrove article states, “Mangroves generate naturally in the mangrove swamp biome.” and “Mangrove trees are found in mangrove swamps, in which they can grow on water or land.” This is consistent with step A (locate mangrove swamp) and step B (identify mangrove trees).

2. Propagules from mangrove trees: The Mangrove article lists among items obtained from mangrove: “Mangrove Propagule (if fully grown, drops when it is broken)” and also notes propagules hang from the canopy: “From a large canopy hangs vines and mangrove propagules.” This matches step B→C, which assumes you can collect mangrove propagules from mangrove trees.

3. Planting locations: The Mangrove article’s Planting section says: “Mangrove propagules can be planted and grown on all of these blocks, even if submerged underwater: Dirt, Grass Block, Coarse Dirt, Podzol, Mycelium, Rooted Dirt, Moss Block, Pale Moss Block, Farmland, Mud, Muddy Mangrove Roots, Clay.” The Fandom version similarly lists valid blocks. This supports step C→D (choosing a planting location) and step D→E (planting the propagule on a chosen block). The flowchart does not claim any invalid block types, so there is no contradiction.

4. Growth from propagules into trees: The Mangrove article describes mangroves as grown from propagules and notes behavior when they grow: “When mangrove trees grow, they convert any mud in the way of the mangrove roots to muddy mangrove roots. They require a minimum clear space of 5×5 to grow… The smallest box in which a tree can be planted is 5×5×5…” and “When a mangrove grows from a propagule, it has a 1% chance of generating a bee nest containing 2-3 bees.” This confirms that planted propagules grow into full mangrove trees, matching step E→F→G.

5. Use of bone meal: While the Mangrove-specific article does not explicitly mention bone meal, the general Tree article states, “Bone meal, when used on a sapling, has a chance of forcing it to grow, so long as all of the normal checks (light, space, dirt, etc.) have passed.” Mangroves are treated as trees grown from propagules (their sapling-equivalent), and there is no statement excluding mangroves from this mechanic. There is no explicit contradiction to using bone meal on propagules to accelerate growth, so under the given rules (only explicit contradictions count) this step is acceptable.

6. Harvesting the tree: The Mangrove article lists all the blocks/items obtainable from mangrove trees (logs, leaves, roots, etc.), which presupposes that once the tree is fully grown it can be harvested. This aligns with the final step G.

No step in the flowchart asserts anything that is explicitly denied in the evidence. All key relationships (biome → trees → propagules → planting → growth into trees) are either directly supported or at least consistent with the provided documents.
